original_abstract: This article provides a hermeneutical exploration of the diverse historical contexts surrounding Question and Answer 45 of the Heidelberg Catechism, centering on the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Beyond merely identifying the intended theological meaning of the text, this study seeks to uncover the underlying hermeneutical frameworks that shape its interpretation. Furthermore, it addresses the tension between scholarly analysis and the lived experience of lay Christians, who often perceive these specialized interpretive methods as foreign or unconventional. Ultimately, the paper demonstrates how divergent hermeneutical processes can lead to vastly different understandings of the same foundational doctrine—outcomes that are, at times, worlds apart.
humanized_abstract: The Heidelberg Catechism’s Q&A 45 has been answered in vastly different ways throughout its long history. Using a hermeneutical lens, this article examines several of the historical contexts in which Q&A 45 has been understood, and in so doing, makes a first foray into uncovering the hermeneutics behind Scripture’s interpretation for the HC. The goal of interpreting the Bible extends beyond merely understanding the theology that Bible authors intended to communicate (exegetical level). Rather, this goal also includes uncovering the hermeneutics employed by those interpreters and examining the extent to which those hermeneutics are understandable to biblical readers. Unfortunately, for many lay readers, this level of technical interpretation does not feel relatable and therefore can be very alienating. Using the resurrection of Christ as a doctrine that all of these varying interpretations have in common, this paper demonstrates how very different hermeneutics can yield understandings of doctrine that are dramatically different from one another.
